text: and looking forward to what God has for us this evening. And, of course, over the last couple of Wednesday nights, kind of entering into 2026, we've had this thought, a longing to see God work. And I've asked this question, and I'd like for you to consider it this evening by way of introduction. Is God working in your life? And you've been to think about that. Is there any evidence, is there something that you can see how God is working in your life? How is God growing you? What is God teaching you? What is He teaching you about Himself? What's He teaching you about you? And what's He teaching you from His Word? And you just begin to see how God is working. How is God using you in the lives of others? We're to invest, intentionally invest in others, and God will use us to be able to share the gospel with folks. God will use us to be able to encourage folks. God will use us to be able to disciple folks. And just begin to think about how God is working. And really as we examine our lives, and we have this idea, this thought, a longing to see God work, I've said this each of the last two Wednesday evenings, I desire to see God work in my life personally. I desire to see God work in your life. I desire to see God work in our church corporately. That God would use us to make a difference in this region where He has placed us in Northern Virginia. And you see all the things that are going on in our world, and I believe that it's obvious that the Lord is coming soon, and we look forward to that. And the days are short. There's much work to be accomplished. There's a world that needs to hear the gospel of the Lord Jesus Christ, and we desperately need to see Him do what only He can do. And I want to remind you, we said this last week, but I want to remind you that God has as much power today as He ever has. Amen? He has as much power today as He ever has. When He created the universe, He didn't lose any of His power. When He parted the Red Sea, He didn't lose any of His power. When He provided manna for all those... He doesn't lose any of His power. He has all power. He is able. And last week, we looked at a portion of Scripture in which the kings were gathered, and we looked at Jehoram and Jehoshaphat and that agreement that was made, and how they went to Elisha, and Elisha delivered that message and of digging the trenches and the pits, and that God would give water. And he said this, it was a light thing. It was a light thing. And we kind of focused on that for a few minutes last Wednesday evening and talking about the idea that it's no big deal for God. It was a light thing. And he said, furthermore, God was also going to give them victory there in that endeavor that they were seeking to have victory. As we come to this thought this evening, we're in 2 Kings chapter number 20, and we find that this portion of Scripture begins with an announcement regarding King Hezekiah. Now, before I get into the text, let me just remind you that Hezekiah, like Jehoshaphat last week, we talked about him being basically a good king. Hezekiah is a king in Judah, and he's a good king. And we will see, if you were to study through his life beyond the chapter here, that unfortunately he was given 15 additional years, and he really didn't use those the best way that he could use those. But we do find that he was basically a good king. He was a man that trusted the Lord. And you think about Rabshakeh and Sennacherib, the king of Assyria, and how they came against Israel and would take Israel captive. And they came against Judah, and Rabshakeh as the general would come and make all of these threats. And Hezekiah trusted the Lord, and Hezekiah prayed, and Hezekiah believed that God was able, and God delivered, and God gave victory, and God defeated the Assyrians. And we see that Hezekiah had experienced, had lived through seeing God work and do great things. We come here to 2 Kings 20, and God will use the prophet Isaiah to deliver a message to Hezekiah. Notice in verse 1 it says, This was Hezekiah sick unto death. And the prophet Isaiah, the son of Amos, came to him and said unto him, Thus saith the Lord. Notice this. Set thine house in order, for thou shalt die and not live. That will wake you up. Amen? I mean, this is an announcement that the Bible says, that Isaiah says, Thus saith the Lord. And this announcement, this is given with regard to Hezekiah. He was sick, and God says through the prophet, Set thine house in order, for thou shalt die and not live. The reality, it's not the main focus of the message this evening, but the reality is that for every one of us, at some point we're going to face death. But we know that it's appointed unto man once to die. And the reality is that death is not the worst thing that could happen to us because to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord. But I want you to notice that as we look through this portion of Scripture and again a longing to see God work, we notice some things about Hezekiah and about the Lord in these next several verses that are noteworthy and I hope that they would help us this evening to encourage us and also to challenge us. I want you to notice first of all with me Hezekiah's prayer. Notice in verses two through four, God gives to us the record of Hezekiah's prayer. He prayed immediately. The Bible says, set thine house in order for thou shalt die and not live. Then he turned his face to the wall and prayed unto the Lord saying. He wants prayer to be our first response, not a last resort.
